title: Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan
summary: Practical guidance for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” within “Navigation · Family · Movement”. It supports clear responsibilities, verified information, visible records, fair priorities, stop conditions, and a workable handover, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.
content:
Practical guidance for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” within “Navigation · Family · Movement”. It supports clear responsibilities, verified information, visible records, fair priorities, stop conditions, and a workable handover, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.

Start with: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Stop condition: Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Reduced fallback: If “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a smaller documented task with one owner, one deadline, and one independent check.
materials: paper map and pencil — for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
official alert and route information — for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
contact and meeting-point card — for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
light, water, and essential medicine — for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
mobility and visibility aids — for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”
steps: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Confirm the reason for movement, official instruction, destination, primary route, fallback route, weather, daylight, and transport limits. Record the result under “Navigation · Family · Movement” before continuing “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan”.

Mark hazards such as fire, smoke, floodwater, damaged bridges, unstable slopes, crowds, poor visibility, and restricted areas. Recheck this point before closing “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Match pace, load, rest, assistance, and transport to children, older adults, disability, illness, animals, and the slowest safe member. Treat any uncertainty here as an unresolved item in “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Leave a departure time, route, destination, group list, check-in schedule, and missed-contact rule with a trusted person. A second person should be able to verify this part of “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan”.

Stop at planned checkpoints, compare real conditions with the map, and turn back or divert before the route becomes trapping or irreversible. Include the outcome of “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in the handover for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
warnings: Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Do not split the group without a written destination, communication rule, responsible adults, and reunification plan. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
common_mistakes: Skipping the opening control makes “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” unreliable: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Treating the result as safe while ignoring this limit is a serious error: Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Failing to document the final handover action leaves the next person without a trustworthy starting point: Stop at planned checkpoints, compare real conditions with the map, and turn back or divert before the route becomes trapping or irreversible. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
alternatives: If “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a smaller documented task with one owner, one deadline, and one independent check.
Use a dated paper note and spoken read-back for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement” when the normal form, device, or communication channel is unavailable.
failure_signs: Stop and reassess “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” when the route loses a safe exit, visibility, communications, daylight margin, water margin, or physical capacity. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Stop and reassess “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” when a member is missing, distressed, injured, exhausted, confused, or unable to continue safely. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
when_not_to_use: Do not continue “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” while the route crosses floodwater, fire, smoke, unstable slopes, fallen wires, restricted zones, darkness without landmarks, or terrain beyond the group’s ability. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

This card is not authorization to cross a torrent, enter a closed road, split the group without a plan, or rely on one phone map and one route. Topic-specific stop check for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan”: also follow this warning: “Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route in “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan”.”. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
short_term: Complete one verified cycle for “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”, record its owner, time, result, unresolved risk, and next review.
long_term: Review “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” after real use in “Navigation · Family · Movement”; replace failed materials, practise the handover, and update thresholds when official guidance or local conditions change.
if_method_fails: If the method described in “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” gives no verifiable result, stop relying on it, isolate the uncertain output, record where it failed, and switch to a smaller documented task with one owner, one deadline, and one independent check.
environment_notes: Adapt the guidance in “Movement checkpoint: route hazard scan” for “Navigation · Family · Movement” to weather, lighting, crowding, accessibility, power, water, transport, and available skill without removing the stop condition or independent check.

title: Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log
summary: Practical guidance for “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log” within “Navigation · Family · Movement”. It supports clear responsibilities, verified information, visible records, fair priorities, stop conditions, and a workable handover, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.
content:
Practical guidance for “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log” within “Navigation · Family · Movement”. It supports clear responsibilities, verified information, visible records, fair priorities, stop conditions, and a workable handover, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.

Start with: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Stop condition: Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route. Apply this boundary to “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Reduced fallback: If “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a smaller documented task with one owner, one deadline, and one independent check.

steps: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log” in “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Confirm the reason for movement, official instruction, destination, primary route, fallback route, weather, daylight, and transport limits. Record the result under “Navigation · Family · Movement” before continuing “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log”.

Mark hazards such as fire, smoke, floodwater, damaged bridges, unstable slopes, crowds, poor visibility, and restricted areas. Recheck this point before closing “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log” for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Match pace, load, rest, assistance, and transport to children, older adults, disability, illness, animals, and the slowest safe member. Treat any uncertainty here as an unresolved item in “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log” for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.

Leave a departure time, route, destination, group list, check-in schedule, and missed-contact rule with a trusted person. A second person should be able to verify this part of “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log”.

Stop at planned checkpoints, compare real conditions with the map, and turn back or divert before the route becomes trapping or irreversible. Include the outcome of “Movement checkpoint: arrival and return log” in the handover for “Navigation · Family · Movement”.
